worth_it_explanation: A lookalike audience is only as good as the seed it is built from, so a tiny source list, a list that mixes strong buyers with one-time visitors, or a percentage chosen without reference to the campaign goal all degrade the audience before a single ad serves. The checks here read in the audience setup itself, where the source, the source size, and the percentage are all visible, so confirming them takes minutes. The fixes are configuration changes, not a tracking rebuild, and a better seed feeds Meta's delivery a cleaner signal from the next flight forward.
intro: A Lookalike Audience reaches new people who resemble a source list, called the seed, by matching Meta's signals against the seed's demographics and behavior. The quality of the result is set by the seed and by the percentage chosen at creation. A seed that is too small, too mixed, or too broad produces a weaker match, and a percentage chosen without reference to the goal trades reach against precision in the wrong direction. These checks confirm that each Lookalike is built from a strong, large-enough seed, that its percentage fits the campaign goal, and that strong first-party lists are actually being used as seeds rather than left idle.

audit_checks.title: Lookalike Audiences are built from a source that is too small or mixes strong and weak signals
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The lookalike inherits whatever pattern the seed contains. A seed under Meta's recommended size or one that blends best customers with one-time visitors gives the model a muddy signal, so the resulting audience resembles an average of everyone rather than the buyers worth finding.

educate.base:
A Lookalike Audience is built from a source list, the seed, and Meta finds new people who share the seed's characteristics. The model can only learn the pattern that the seed actually contains, so the seed's size and consistency set the ceiling on the result.

Meta recommends a seed of more than 1,000 people, and the seed must contain at least 100 people from a single country before it can be used at all. A seed near the floor still works, but it gives the model fewer examples to generalize from, so the audience leans on a thin pattern. A larger, consistent seed produces a tighter match.

Consistency matters as much as size. A seed that blends high-value buyers with one-time visitors, newsletter subscribers, and abandoned carts asks the model to find people who resemble all of them at once, which averages out to people who resemble no group in particular. A seed scoped to a single strong signal, for example purchasers in the last 180 days or the top quartile by value, gives the model a clear target.

A few signs point to a weak seed:
  • The source Custom Audience is at or below Meta's 1,000-person guidance
  • The seed mixes buyers, leads, and general site visitors in one list
  • The seed is a broad pixel-based audience, e.g., all website visitors, rather than a converting segment
You may want to confirm both the size and the makeup of each Lookalike's source before judging the audience. A seed can clear the size floor and still under-deliver because of what it mixes together.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the strongest seeds are usually purchaser lists segmented by recency or value, for example buyers in the last 180 days or repeat buyers, rather than a single list of everyone who reached the cart. You may want to confirm the seed is scoped to actual purchasers and is large enough to clear Meta's guidance because a store's all-visitors audience dilutes the buying signal the Lookalike is meant to find.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the seed is often a form-submission Custom Audience, and quality varies sharply between raw leads and qualified or closed ones. You may want to seed the Lookalike from the closest signal to revenue you can build, for example a list of leads that became customers uploaded as a Custom Audience, rather than every form fill, so the model resembles buyers and not browsers.

investigate.intro: Open each Lookalike Audience, identify its source, and check the source's size and makeup against Meta's guidance.

audiences_manager.label: Audiences (Meta Business Suite)
steps: In Meta Business Suite, open 'Business settings', then 'Audiences', and filter to the Lookalike Audiences in use.
For each Lookalike, note its source audience and the percentage shown in its name or details.

Open each source Custom Audience and read its approximate size and the rule that defines it. Note whether it is at or below 1,000 people and whether the rule scopes it to one signal, e.g., purchasers, or blends several.
Record any Lookalike whose source is at or below Meta's 1,000-person guidance or whose source mixes buyers, leads, and general visitors in one list.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Lookalike Audience, the source name, the approximate source size, and whether the source is scoped to a single strong signal or mixes several. A Lookalike whose source clears Meta's 1,000-person guidance and is scoped to one converting signal passes. A Lookalike whose source is at or below the guidance, or blends buyers with general visitors, is a fail recorded with the Lookalike name and the source at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the source name, approximate size, and makeup for each Lookalike Audience above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Lookalike Audiences on {{site_name}} are built from a source that is too small or that blends strong and weak signals. A Lookalike can only resemble the pattern in its seed, so a thin or mixed source gives Meta's delivery a muddy target and the audience drifts toward an average of everyone rather than the buyers worth finding. {{voice_recommend}} rebuilding the affected Lookalikes from a larger source that clears Meta's guidance of more than 1,000 people and is scoped to a single strong signal, for example recent purchasers or the highest-value customers. After the change, confirm the new source size and rule in the Audiences view before the next flight.

audit_checks.title: Lookalike percentage does not fit the campaign goal
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The percentage sets the trade between precision and reach. A 1% Lookalike resembles the seed most closely but is small, while a 10% Lookalike is large but loose. A percentage chosen without reference to the goal sends a prospecting campaign too narrow or a precision campaign too wide, and delivery spends against the wrong end of the trade.

educate.base:
When a Lookalike Audience is created, Meta asks for a percentage from 1 to 10 of the population in the chosen country. A 1% Lookalike is the slice that most closely resembles the seed, and it is the smallest. Each step up widens the audience and loosens the resemblance, so a 10% Lookalike is the largest and the least similar to the seed.

Neither end is correct on its own. The right percentage depends on what the campaign is trying to do and how much budget has to spend. A precision campaign with a tight budget and a strong seed benefits from a 1% to 2% Lookalike, where the resemblance is highest. A prospecting campaign that needs scale, or one whose 1% audience is too small to exit the learning phase, benefits from a wider 3% to 6% range.

A mismatch shows up in two directions. A wide percentage on a precision goal spends against people who only loosely resemble the seed, which dilutes the result the seed was meant to produce. A narrow percentage on a scale goal starves delivery, so the campaign struggles to leave the learning phase or exhausts the audience and repeats impressions.

Meta's broader audience tools change this picture. With Advantage+ audience, Meta treats a Lookalike as a suggestion rather than a hard boundary and looks beyond it, so the percentage matters less than it does in original audience setups. You may want to note whether each campaign uses a fixed Lookalike or Advantage+ audience before judging the percentage because the percentage carries different weight in each.

A few signs point to a mismatched percentage:
  • A precision or retention goal running on a 6% to 10% Lookalike
  • A scale or prospecting goal running on a 1% Lookalike too small to exit learning
  • The same percentage applied across every campaign regardless of goal
You may want to confirm the goal of each campaign alongside the Lookalike percentage it uses because the same percentage can be right for one campaign and wrong for the next.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a retention or high-value prospecting push usually wants the tightest resemblance, so a 1% to 2% Lookalike from a purchaser seed fits, while a broad seasonal scale push can support a wider 3% to 5% range. You may want to confirm the percentage matches the push rather than carrying one default percentage across the whole account.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, audiences are often smaller than a store's, so a 1% Lookalike in a single country can be too small to leave the learning phase and stabilize cost per lead. You may want to consider a 2% to 4% Lookalike when the 1% audience is starving delivery, and watch whether cost per lead steadies once the audience is large enough to learn.

investigate.intro: For each campaign using a Lookalike Audience, read the Lookalike percentage and check it against the campaign's goal and audience size.

ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: In Ads Manager, open the ad sets that target Lookalike Audiences and note the Lookalike percentage each one uses, shown in the audience name, e.g., '1% Lookalike'.
For each ad set, note the campaign objective and whether the goal is precision, e.g., retention or high-value prospecting, or scale, e.g., broad prospecting.
Note whether the ad set uses a fixed Lookalike or Advantage+ audience. Advantage+ audience looks beyond the Lookalike and changes how much the percentage matters.
Record any ad set whose percentage runs against its goal, for example a 10% Lookalike on a precision goal or a 1% Lookalike too small to exit the learning phase.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad set targeting a Lookalike, the percentage used, the campaign goal, and whether the ad set uses a fixed Lookalike or Advantage+ audience. An ad set whose percentage fits its goal, or that uses Advantage+ audience where the percentage carries less weight, passes. An ad set running a wide percentage on a precision goal or a 1% percentage too small for a scale goal is a fail recorded with the ad set name and the mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the percentage, goal, and audience type for each Lookalike ad set above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} run a Lookalike percentage that does not fit the campaign goal. The percentage sets the trade between resemblance and reach, so a wide Lookalike on a precision goal spends against loose matches, and a narrow Lookalike on a scale goal starves delivery and struggles to leave the learning phase. {{voice_recommend}} matching the percentage to each goal, for example a 1% to 2% Lookalike for precision and a wider range for scale, and noting where Advantage+ audience already looks beyond the Lookalike so the percentage carries less weight. After the change, watch the affected campaigns through their next learning phase to confirm delivery stabilizes.

audit_checks.title: A strong first-party customer list exists but is not used as a Lookalike seed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A customer list that already converts is the strongest seed available, and an account that prospects only on interest and demographic targeting while that list sits idle leaves its best signal unused. The prospecting reaches people who resemble Meta's broad categories rather than people who resemble actual buyers.

educate.base:
The strongest seed for a Lookalike Audience is a list of people who already converted because the model then finds new people who resemble buyers rather than people who resemble a broad interest category. An account that holds a strong first-party list, for example a customer export or a list of qualified leads, but prospects only on interests and demographics is leaving that signal unused.

A first-party customer list reaches Meta as a Custom Audience, uploaded in the Audiences view or sent through the Conversions API and a connected dataset. Once the list is a Custom Audience that clears the size floor, it can seed a Lookalike. The gap is usually not the data, given the business has the customers, but the step of turning that data into a seed.

Two situations point to an unused seed. The account has a customer Custom Audience present but no Lookalike built from it, so the strongest list is used only for retargeting and exclusion. Or the business holds customer data offline, e.g., in a CRM, that was never uploaded as a Custom Audience, so it cannot seed anything.

A few signs suggest a strong seed is going unused:
  • Prospecting ad sets target only interests and demographics with no Lookalike present
  • A customer Custom Audience exists but no Lookalike is built from it
  • Customer data lives in a CRM and was never uploaded as a Custom Audience
You may want to confirm both what first-party lists the business holds and which of them seed a Lookalike, given that a strong list that only retargets is doing a fraction of the work it could.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the purchaser list is usually the highest-value seed available, and it can be segmented further by value or recency before it seeds a Lookalike. You may want to confirm a Lookalike is built from purchasers, not only from site visitors, so prospecting resembles people who bought rather than people who browsed.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the closed-won or qualified-lead list is the seed closest to revenue, and it often lives in a CRM rather than in Meta. You may want to upload that list as a Custom Audience and seed a Lookalike from it, so prospecting resembles leads that became customers rather than every form fill.

investigate.intro: Inventory the first-party lists the account holds, then check whether each strong list seeds a Lookalike or only retargets.

audiences_manager.label: Audiences (Meta Business Suite)
steps: In Meta Business Suite, open 'Business settings', then 'Audiences', and list the Custom Audiences that represent customers or qualified leads, e.g., a customer-list upload or a purchase-event audience.
For each strong Custom Audience, note whether a Lookalike Audience is built from it.
In Ads Manager, open the prospecting ad sets and note whether any target only interests and demographics with no Lookalike present.

Ask the business whether customer data sits in a CRM that was never uploaded as a Custom Audience. Record any strong list that exists but seeds no Lookalike, and any prospecting that runs without a Lookalike where a strong seed is available.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the strong first-party lists the account holds, whether each seeds a Lookalike, and whether prospecting runs on interests and demographics alone. An account whose strongest converting lists each seed a Lookalike passes. An account that holds a strong customer or qualified-lead list, in Meta or in a CRM, that seeds no Lookalike while prospecting runs on broad targeting is a fail recorded with the unused list named.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the strong first-party lists the account holds and whether each seeds a Lookalike to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} holds a strong first-party customer list that is not used as a Lookalike seed, so prospecting reaches people who resemble broad interest categories rather than people who resemble actual buyers. A converting customer list is the strongest seed available, and leaving it idle while prospecting on interests alone forgoes the account's best signal. {{voice_recommend}} uploading the strongest converting list as a Custom Audience where it is not already in Meta, building a Lookalike from it, and testing that Lookalike against the current interest-based prospecting. After launch, compare cost per result between the Lookalike and the existing prospecting to confirm the seed is pulling its weight.
